ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for Dal Tadka; 'lentil dish' (FOODON_03311434) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC for cooked lentils is used as the closest defensible anchor; no specific Dal Tadka entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Dal Tadka; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: may contain traces of gluten (asafoetida preparations)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Dal Tadka is approximately 250 g (one bowl or katori), typically accompanied by rice or Indian bread such as roti or naan.

nutrient_source.title: Dal, red gram, whole, cooked (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) entry for cooked red gram (toor dal) used as the primary nutrient basis. Values are adapted to reflect the cooked, tempered preparation (Dal Tadka) which includes added ghee/oil and spices. Sodium is elevated relative to plain cooked dal to account for salt added during restaurant preparation. Fat is adjusted upward to account for ghee or oil used in the tadka tempering step. All adaptations are conservative and within the range reported for comparable restaurant-style dal preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Lentils, red, boiled (Atkinson et al.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dal Tadka is prepared from toor dal (yellow pigeon peas / red gram). The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 29 for boiled red lentils (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31:2281–2283). Toor dal and red lentils are both legumes with similar starch structure and fibre content; GI of 29 is applied as the closest defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (29 × 28.75) / 100 ≈ 8.3, rounded to 8.4 to account for slight carbohydrate variation in restaurant preparation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of Dal Tadka contributes approximately 800 mg sodium, which represents about 35% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ≈ 29) and high dietary fibre from lentils
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Tadka has a low GI of approximately 29 and a glycemic load of approximately 8 per standard serving, making it a suitable legume-based dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with controlled portions of rice or bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre and plant protein from lentils; moderate saturated fat from ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal Tadka provides beneficial soluble fibre and plant protein that support LDL cholesterol reduction; however, the ghee-based tadka contributes saturated fat (~1.4 g per 100 g), so individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer preparations using unsaturated oil instead of ghee.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Tadka is a classic North Indian cooked lentil dish made from yellow lentils — most commonly toor dal (pigeon peas) or chana dal — that are boiled until soft and then finished with a hot tempering (tadka) of ghee or oil infused with garlic, cumin seeds, mustard seeds, dried red chillies, turmeric, and asafoetida. The dish has a smooth, slightly thick consistency with a warm golden-yellow colour. The aroma is distinctly smoky and garlicky from the tadka, and the flavour is earthy, mildly spiced, and savoury. Dal Tadka is a staple of North Indian home cooking and restaurant menus across India and the Indian diaspora, including Singapore, where it is served at Indian restaurants such as Curry Village, Mirch Masalah, and Khushboo. It is typically served with steamed basmati rice, jeera rice, roti, or naan. Nutritionally, Dal Tadka is a good source of plant protein (~5.8 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (~3.1 g per 100 g), folate, iron, and potassium. Its low glycemic index (~29) makes it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is vegetarian and, when prepared with oil rather than ghee, can be made vegan. Sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations due to added salt, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Dal Tadka?
Is Dal Tadka good for diabetics?
What lentils are used in Dal Tadka?
What is the difference between Dal Tadka and Dal Makhani?
Is Dal Tadka high in protein?
Can I eat Dal Tadka if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for cooked toor dal, with adjustments for restaurant-style preparation including added ghee and salt. No exact match for restaurant-prepared Dal Tadka exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using red lentils as the closest defensible proxy. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked red gram (toor dal) data with conservative upward adjustments for fat (+1.5 g per 100 g) and sodium (+200 mg per 100 g) to reflect restaurant-style tadka preparation. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as Dal Tadka is a plant-based dish; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods with no animal-derived ingredients other than ghee, which contributes negligible B12. Confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN plant food entries. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in ghee-based preparations per ICMR-NIN and USDA data. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 7. GI of 29 is an analogous estimate based on red lentils from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). 8. The food_name has been normalised to 'Dal Tadka' (canonical English form) from the input 'Curry Village-Dal Tadka', which contained a restaurant prefix. 9. Served at three Singapore restaurants: Mirch Masalah (Boat Quay), Khushboo – The Fragrance of India (Boat Quay), and Curry Village – Authentic North Indian Cuisine.
